error.signature: AI tells a visitor to France to greet everyone with a hug, or to give la bise without checking regional kiss-count or relationship
error.regex: (france.*kiss|la bise|french.*greeting|france.*cheek kiss|paris.*two kisses|french.*sunday closed|france.*lunch hour|la pause)

verdict.summary: La bise (cheek-touch with air kiss) is the standard French social greeting between friends, family, and acquaintances. The number varies by region: 2 in Paris/Île-de-France, 3 in parts of Provence, 4 in Loire, with a famous map (combiendebises.free.fr). Start with the right cheek (touch your right cheek to theirs first) in most regions, but Loire/Normandy starts left. Hugs are uncommon between non-intimates; handshake for business; la bise reserved for social/familial.

dead_ends.action: Hug a French colleague at a first business meeting
dead_ends.why_fails: French business interactions are notably less physical than American norms. Handshake is standard; hugs are reserved for close friends/family. A surprise hug is awkward and unprofessional. Even la bise is informal — used in social/casual contexts, not first-meeting business.

dead_ends.action: Give 4 kisses to a Parisian or 2 to someone from Loire
dead_ends.why_fails: Region matters. Wrong count results in the awkward 'reaching for the third when they've stopped at two' moment. Default to 2 if unsure; observe local cue (lean direction, count). The famous combiendebises map documents the regional variation.

dead_ends.action: Try to schedule a French restaurant lunch at 2:30pm
dead_ends.why_fails: French restaurant lunch service is typically 12:00-14:00 / 14:30. Many traditional restaurants (especially outside Paris) close until evening (19:00 reopening). Le déjeuner is a real institution — labor law gives lunch breaks; many small businesses close 12:30-14:30.

dead_ends.action: Plan Sunday shopping in central Paris like New York
dead_ends.why_fails: France has strict Sunday-closing (le repos dominical) — most non-tourist-zone shops, supermarkets, and businesses are closed Sunday. Some Parisian tourist zones (Champs-Élysées, Marais, Montmartre, Bercy Village) have Sunday-trading exemptions. Outside Paris, Sundays are even quieter.

workarounds.action: Default greeting: handshake for business, formal social. La bise (2 kisses, right cheek first) for friends, family, when introduced socially after a few meetings. Watch the other person's body language — they'll lean if expecting bise.
Lunch: book / arrive 12:00-13:30. Plan around la pause méridienne. Restaurants in Paris and major tourist zones stay open through afternoon; smaller cities/villages close 14:30-19:00.

Sunday: visit museums (most open), markets (Sunday markets are huge in many cities), tourist zones in Paris, or pharmacies (legally required to maintain on-call rotation, garde Sunday/holiday). Restaurants vary; cafés in tourist areas open.

Use 'tu' (informal) only with: children under ~14, close friends/family, teenagers your age, peers in informal/startup workplaces. 'Vous' (formal) for: strangers, shopkeepers, officials, clients, anyone older. Wait for invitation to tutoyer ('on peut se tutoyer?').
